task: I found a wallet left behind in a hotel room and I want to handle it the right way without overcomplicating it. Can you help me draft a simple note for the front desk and a quick checklist for what to do next?

prefs.explanation: She comes across as practical and unshowy, so overly polished or stiff language would likely feel unnatural to her. A neutral or slightly casual tone fits better than formal phrasing. She probably wants help that sounds like a real person, not a scripted office memo.

Her life is built around getting things done efficiently, not lingering on extra explanation. She would likely prefer concise answers that get to the point quickly, with more detail only if needed. Long-winded responses would probably feel like wasted time.

She values honesty and practical clarity, so she would likely appreciate blunt but respectful communication. Softening every point too much could feel evasive to her. She seems to do best when people say exactly what they mean.

prefs.short: Best served by simple lists and steps
prefs.explanation: Her routine-oriented style suggests she would respond well to checklists, bullet points, or clear step-by-step guidance. That kind of format matches how she handles work problems and caregiving tasks. Dense paragraphs would be less helpful than something easy to scan.

She is grounded and practical, so plain language would fit her better than technical or overly academic phrasing. Jargon would likely slow her down unless it is necessary and explained simply. She seems to prefer communication that is clear, direct, and easy to use right away.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a simple, plain version you can use.

Front desk note:
Wallet found in room [room number].
It was left behind after checkout/cleaning.
Wallet appears to contain [cash/cards/ID if known].
It has been turned in to [front desk/lost and found/manager].
Guest was [not aware / aware if you spoke with them].

Quick checklist:
- Pick up wallet with gloves or a tissue if needed
- Do not go through it more than necessary
- Check room number and note where it was found
- Write down what it is and any obvious contents
- Turn it in to front desk or lost and found right away
- Let the manager know if your hotel requires that
- If the guest is still around and allowed by policy, notify front desk only
- Make sure your note is clear and simple

If you want, I can also make this into a super short one-line handoff note you can copy fast.

per_pref.reason: Uses a plain, natural tone: "Absolutely — here’s a simple, plain version you can use." No stiff or overly polished language.
Gets to the point quickly with a short note and checklist, and even offers an optional shorter version: "If you want, I can also make this into a super short one-line handoff note."
Direct and practical throughout, e.g. "Do not go through it more than necessary" and "Turn it in to front desk or lost and found right away."
Uses a clear note plus bullet-point checklist, which is easy to scan and action-oriented.
Plain English with no jargon or fancy wording; phrases like "simple, plain version" and "Make sure your note is clear and simple" match this preference well.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — that works. Here’s a plain version you can use:

**Front desk note:**  
Wallet found in room [number] during cleaning.  
Turned in to front desk/lost and found at [time].  
Contents not fully checked.

**Checklist:**  
- Pick it up carefully  
- Note the room number  
- Don’t dig through it  
- Turn it in right away  
- Write down the time and where it was found  

**One-line log version:**  
Wallet found in room [number] and turned in at [time].

If you want, I can also make it even shorter for a quick handwritten note.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, natural language like "Yep — that works" and "Here’s a plain version you can use," which fits a no-fuss, slightly casual tone.
Gives the short version first with a compact note and checklist, then offers more only if needed: "If you want, I can also make it even shorter."
Direct and practical: "Turn it in right away," "Don’t dig through it," and "Contents not fully checked" are blunt, clear instructions without fluff.
Uses simple lists and steps, including a labeled "Front desk note," a bullet-point "Checklist," and a "One-line log version."
Stays in plain English with easy-to-use wording like "wallet found," "turned in," and "write down the time," avoiding jargon or fancy phrasing.
